industryRead.buyerProfile: Manufacturing and quality engineers specifying tooling by thread form, size, material, and tolerance — technical, spec-led, RFQ-driven.
industryRead.channelNotes: No SKUs: every order is a specification plus a quote. Licensed thread systems are capabilities, not products, and must never be merchandised as catalogue items.

claims.text: Engineering buyers split into known-item (they already have a drawing/part) and exploratory (scoping capability); both must be served — drawing/profile submission AND guided spec entry.

sources.title: NN/g — E-Commerce UX: Search incl. Faceted Search

claims.text: Made-to-order tooling has no price tag; the conversion event is a single fully-specified quote request, and B2B buyers prefer self-serve up to that point (~17% of time with suppliers; a majority prefer rep-free).
A comparison view of thread forms and tolerance classes helps engineers decide faster — comparison is a critical pre-decision activity.

sources.title: NN/g — Comparison Tables for Products, Services, and Features

claims.text: Tiering lead-time / capability options (standard, expedited, full-custom) guides specification and anchors value.

sources.title: HBR — The Good-Better-Best Approach to Pricing

claims.reviewerNote: Good-better-best is well-evidenced for products; applying it to bespoke-tooling lead-time tiers is by extension — confirm with Meridian before pitching.

recommendations.title: Offer both 'submit a drawing' and 'guide me by spec'
recommendations.rationale: Some engineers arrive with a STEP/DXF and a part in hand; others are scoping a job. Serve both — a submission path for the known-item buyer, a guided spec flow for the explorer — and neither has to wear the other's shoes.

recommendations.title: Single-spec quote request as the conversion event
recommendations.rationale: There's no checkout for bespoke tooling. Capture the fully-specified ask — thread form, size, material, tolerance, volume — as a qualified RFQ while the buyer is still self-serving.

recommendations.title: Add a thread-form / tolerance comparison view
recommendations.rationale: A clear comparison of forms and tolerance classes (and standard vs expedited vs full-custom lead-time tiers) speeds specification and anchors the premium option.

exemplars.name: Misumi
exemplars.why: Configure-and-quote at scale — the cart is a specification, not a checkout.

exemplars.name: McMaster-Carr
exemplars.why: Serves part-number known-item lookups and parametric browse side by side.

valuePitch.headline: Turn a capability brochure into a spec-and-quote engine.
valuePitch.narrative: Meridian's brochure tells engineers what's possible; it doesn't let them DO anything. The configurator preview turns capability prose into a guided specification — thread form, size, material, tolerance — that ends in a single, fully-specified quote request. Engineers who already have a drawing submit it; those scoping a job get guided. Both arrive at a qualified RFQ carrying its own requirements, instead of a 'contact us' form.

topMoves.move: Guided spec entry + drawing submission (serve both buyer modes)
topMoves.why: Engineering buyers split into known-item and exploratory; serving both is standard best practice for technical catalogues.

topMoves.move: Single-spec quote request as the conversion event
topMoves.why: Made-to-order has no checkout; capture the fully-specified ask while the buyer self-serves ~83% of the journey.
Comparison is a critical pre-decision activity; a clear table speeds specification.

valuePitch.nextStep: Book a 30-minute call — we'll demo the guided spec flow against Meridian's own capabilities.
